exemplar.title:
To what extent is sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County influenced
by wind process, soil acidity, and human and environmental conditions?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent is sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County influenced
by wind process, soil acidity, and human and environmental conditions?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical with explicit theoretical links and testable hypotheses.
Site selection is precise (Mambrui dunes, distances from Malindi and Mombasa) and feasible for primary data collection.
Background on sediment sources and processes is coherent and supports the research design.
weaknesses: Maps are drawn with required conventions but suffer from inaccuracies (distorted coastline, inconsistent symbol sizes, missing scale bar).
Background theoretical discussion omits key dune-succession models and lacks citations.
Humanâenvironment variables are not fully specified in the theory section.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The question and context meet topâband standards: clear geographical focus, strong theoretical linkage, and feasible, precise location. Mapping conventions are present but execution errors prevent a perfect score. Overall, a mark of 3 reflects excellent performance with minor mapping and theoreticalâbackground shortcomings.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods for pH, wind speed/direction, and vegetation cover are clearly described stepâbyâstep and relate directly to the research question.
Use of digital instruments and multiple trials enhances data reliability.
Transect design (15 sites) yields a sufficient dataset for analysis.
weaknesses: Sampling intervals vary without justification, risking bias in trend detection.
Calibration procedures for the pH meter and wind instruments are not detailed.
Random quadrant placement and single seed point sampling are inadequately justified.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation methods are well described and yield usable data, placing this work squarely in band 2. Limited theoretical alignment, inconsistent sampling decisions, and lack of calibration detail prevent a topâband score.

strengths: Dataset (pH, wind speed, vegetation cover across 15 sites) is directly relevant to all hypotheses and largely sufficient for analysis.
Appropriate presentation techniques (line graphs, bar charts, scatter plots) highlight key patterns.
Overall, conventions (titles, axes, units) are followed correctly in most figures.
weaknesses: Several axes are mislabelled or have inconsistent units and captions.
Error bars and measures of uncertainty are missing from graphical presentations.
Occasional inconsistencies in significant figures across tables and charts.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data selection and presentation are appropriate and mostly effective, meriting the top of the 3â4 band. Frequent, minor convention errors keep this from the highest band, so a score of 4 accurately reflects quality and treatment of information.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Use of descriptive statistics alongside Pearsonâs correlation demonstrates solid statistical application.
Trends and anomalies are interpreted in relation to succession theory, showing an understanding of geomorphological processes.
All hypotheses are addressed with clear links between data patterns and the research question.
weaknesses: No significance testing, pâvalues, or confidence intervals accompany the correlation coefficients.
Linkages to humanâenvironment interactions and swashâalignment theory are mentioned only briefly.
Multiâvariable statistical analyses are not attempted, leaving some analytical gaps.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis goes beyond basic description by applying Pearsonâs correlation and interpreting anomalies in light of theory. While lacking significance testing and deeper theoretical integration, the work meets the descriptors for the lower end of band 5â6. A score of 5 acknowledges strong statistical use with room for more comprehensive treatment.

strengths: Conclusion clearly restates and confirms each hypothesis based on observed correlations.
Arguments are supported by the analysis presented, satisfying the requirement for a fully supported conclusion.
weaknesses: No reflection on study limitations or broader geomorphological implications.
Conclusion is largely a summary without extending to future research or applications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear, fully supported by the analysis, and meets all criteria for a full band-2 mark. Its lack of depth beyond summarizing results is appropriate for this level.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant strengths (e.g., multiple trials, team roles) and weaknesses (e.g., cyclone influence, equipment limits).
Improvement suggestions (standardized intervals, multiple instruments, extended sampling) directly address identified weaknesses.
Some discussion of how changes would improve data quality shows understanding of methodological impact.
weaknesses: Evaluation could be more comprehensive across all methodological stages.
Impact of suggested improvements is described only briefly rather than in detail.
No quantification of how limitations affected specific data points or trends.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strengths and weaknesses are wellâjustified and relevant improvement suggestions are provided with some discussion of impact, fitting the descriptors for a topâband evaluation. A score of 3 reflects a comprehensive and relevant evaluation with scope for deeper impact analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two hand-drawn maps with scale, north arrow and key are provided, but accuracy is modest (e.g. distorted coastline, inconsistent symbol sizes).  Mapping conventions are observed but not entirely precise, so the work best fits the middle band where âmap included but with excess detail or lacking some conventions.â

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focuses on coastal dune formation and explicitly references wind, soil pH and human-environment conditions.  It is linked to syllabus concepts (sediment transport, succession) and is immediately followed by three testable hypotheses, showing that the question lends itself to hypothesis formation.  This aligns well with the top-band descriptor.

Line graphs, bar charts, scatter plots and a correlation matrix are appropriate to the variable types and allow patterns to be seen.  They are functional but labels are occasionally confusing (wind speed graph captioned âMean pHâ), so the work fits the âappropriate techniques usedâ band rather than âmost effective.â

Digital instruments, three trials and a 15-site transect show reasonable implementation and yield usable data.  Yet sampling interval changes are only partly justified and quality-control (e.g. calibration, triangulation) is limited, so reliability is moderate rather than âhigh.â  Band 2 is therefore the best fit.

Descriptive statistics are complemented by Pearsonâs correlation, giving an appropriate basic statistical treatment.  No confidence limits or significance testing are attempted, so analysis is solid but not âcomprehensiveâ; the upper end of the 3â4 band is the best match.

The student links falling pH and wind speed trends to dune succession theory and discusses anomalies, but connections to the swash alignment theory and human factors remain brief.  Interpretation is therefore competent yet not exhaustive, aligning with the middle band.

The student identifies Mambrui dunes, gives distance from Malindi and Mombasa, and includes justification for site choice.  Primary data collection along a transect is clearly feasible and actually carried out.  The level of locational specificity (named beach section, measured distances) meets the âprecise locationâ requirement.

Strengths and weaknesses are outlined with some explanation (e.g., cyclone influence, instrument accuracy).  Discussion is relevant but not comprehensive across all methodological stages, so the middle band (2 marks) is the closest fit.

The dataset (pH, wind speed, vegetation cover across 15 points) is largely relevant and adequate to address all three hypotheses.  Minor gaps exist (no sediment grain size, limited temporal coverage) so it does not reach the highest sufficiency standard; a lower value within the 3â4 band is most appropriate.

The conclusion restates each hypothesis and supports them with reference to observed correlations, satisfying the requirement for a clear, supported conclusion.  It does not extend beyond summary, but still earns the full two marks available.

All three hypotheses are analysed, but temporal limitations and lack of multi-variable statistics leave noticeable gaps.  Evidence is provided for main claims, giving a solid but not fully complete analysis, appropriate to band 3â4 with a mid-value score.

The methods for pH, wind speed/direction and vegetation cover are described step-by-step and are clearly related to the research question.  However, explanations of why each variable is important are brief and theoretical alignment is limited; hence the strand sits comfortably in band 2 rather than the comprehensive top band.

Most figures include titles, axes and units, but there are several convention errors (e.g., mis-labelled axes, inconsistent significant figures, missing error bars).  These frequent minor issues position the work at the top of the 1â2 band.

Improvement suggestions (equal intervals, multiple instruments, longer time frame) are relevant and linked to identified weaknesses, though impacts are only briefly explained.  This aligns with the descriptor for band 2 rather than the detailed expectations of band 3.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, referencing wind processes, soil acidity, and humanâenvironmental factors, and it supports hypothesis formation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Consider refining âhuman and environmental conditionsâ by specifying which human activities or environmental variables will be measured and why.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The table of contents lacks page numbers for subsections, making navigation and reference to specific sections difficult.
The background overview introduces coastal processes but lacks citations and depth in dune-formation theory; include key models and references.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The background effectively describes sediment sources (glacial fill, riverine input) and processes driving dune development.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The Kenya location map is clear but the coastline appears distorted and it lacks a scale bar to validate distances.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: In the detailed Malindi map, symbol sizes vary inconsistently and there is no grid or coordinate reference.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The three hypotheses are stated clearly and in a testable form, supporting the research design.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Provide a theoretical rationale for each hypothesis, explicitly linking to processes (e.g. salinity influence on pH, succession theory).
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The pH procedure is well-structured, with three trials and cleaning protocols, enhancing measurement reliability.
Include calibration steps or reference solutions for the digital pH meter to further validate the pH measurements.
The wind equipment list is appropriate, including anemometer and compass for direction.
The geographic theory section cites the swash-alignment model but omits core dune succession theories (e.g. embryo to grey dune).
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The random sampling description is vague and does not justify how the initial point represents the whole dune system.
Changing intervals from 10âm to 100âm along the transect lacks justification; this could bias detection of gradual trends.
The vegetation rationale describes ecological importance but would benefit from linking cover metrics to specific soil and wind gradients.
Randomly throwing the quadrant may lead to uneven sampling; consider systematic or stratified placements.
Acknowledging three trials and outlier exclusion shows awareness of error reduction and data quality.
Raising the anemometer by height of a person introduces inconsistency; use a fixed pole to standardize sensor height.
Assuming the one starting point is representative of all coastal conditions is risky; justify or include multiple seed points.
Table 1 compiles mean pH, wind speed, and vegetation across 15 sites, providing a solid dataset for analysis.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Caption âdata collected in averagesâ is vague; specify variables and ensure axis labels and units are consistent.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 8 shows pH vs distance but caption and axis labels should match; clarify axes and highlight plateau at 100â500 m.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Vegetation analysis links zonation well but would benefit from addressing anomalies and human-environment interactions explicitly.
Including the correct Pearson formula demonstrates appropriate statistical application.
The correlation coefficient lacks a p-value or confidence interval; include significance testing to strengthen analysis.
Interpretation of strong negative correlation between pH and vegetation is well linked to succession theory and soil acidification.
The conclusion clearly answers the research question and confirms hypotheses based on observed correlations.
The conclusion restates results but omits discussion of limitations or broader geomorphological implications.
Evaluation identifies digital equipment and team roles as strengths, accurately reflecting on data collection quality.
Weakness section rightly notes one-day sampling and cyclone influence; consider quantifying how wind variability affected readings.
Improvement suggestions propose interval standardization and multiple instruments; great relevance to identified weaknesses.
Enhance suggestions by indicating expected impact on data precision or how extended duration sampling would alter results.
